title: Check if tree is full
description: Determine whether a binary tree is a full binary tree where every node has either 0 or 2 children

examples.input: Tree: root(1) with left(2) and right(3); node 2 has left(4) and right(5); node 3 has left(6) and right(7); all leaves have no children
examples.explanation:
1. Start at root(1): has 2 children (2 and 3) → check both subtrees
2. Check left subtree at node(2): has 2 children (4 and 5) → check both
3. Check node(4): leaf (0 children) → returns true
4. Check node(5): leaf (0 children) → returns true
5. Node(2) receives true AND true → returns true
6. Check right subtree at node(3): has 2 children (6 and 7) → check both
7. Check node(6): leaf → returns true
8. Check node(7): leaf → returns true
9. Node(3) receives true AND true → returns true
10. Root(1) receives true AND true → returns true

examples.input: Tree: root(1) with left(2) and right(3); node 2 has left(4) only; node 3 has no children
examples.explanation:
1. Start at root(1): has 2 children (2 and 3) → check both
2. Check node(2): has only left child (4) → 1 child → returns false immediately
3. Root receives false AND (whatever from right subtree) → false
4. Algorithm may still check node(3) but result is already determined as false

approaches.name: Approach 1: Recursive DFS Check
approaches.summary: Perform a depth-first traversal recursively, checking at each node if it has either 0 or 2 children; return false if any node violates this condition. This approach leverages recursion to naturally handle the tree structure and propagate results up.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(h) where h is the height of the tree (due to recursion stack)

ladders.title: Full Problem — Recursive DFS Check for Full Binary Tree
ladders.desc: Complete recursive solution to determine if a binary tree is full (every node has 0 or 2 children).
ladders.explanation:
This solution implements a recursive depth-first traversal that checks each node's children configuration. The intuition is that a full binary tree's property must hold at every node, so we can recursively verify each subtree and combine results. Starting from the root, we check if the current node has either 0 children (leaf) or exactly 2 children. If it has 1 child, we immediately return false. For nodes with 2 children, we recursively check both left and right subtrees, returning true only if both subtrees are full. The base case handles null nodes (empty trees are considered full by convention). This approach naturally follows the tree structure, with recursion handling the traversal and result propagation. Time complexity is O(n) as we visit each node once. Space complexity is O(h) for the recursion stack, where h is the tree height. This connects to L1 by implementing the complete recursive function described there. Common mistakes include not handling null nodes properly, forgetting to check both subtrees when a node has 2 children, or incorrectly returning early. Real code pattern: define a recursive function that checks node's children and recurses appropriately.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Define function isFullTree(node):
2. If node is null: return true (empty tree is full)
3. If node has exactly 0 children (both left and right are null): return true
4. If node has exactly 1 child (either left or right is null but not both): return false
5. If node has 2 children (both left and right are not null):
   - return isFullTree(node.left) AND isFullTree(node.right)
6. Call isFullTree(root) to start the check

examples.input: Tree: root(1) with left(2) and right(3); node 2 has left(4) and right(5); node 3 has left(6) and right(7); all leaves have no children
examples.trace:
1. Start at root(1): has 2 children (2 and 3) → check both subtrees
2. Check left subtree at node(2): has 2 children (4 and 5) → check both
3. Check node(4): leaf (0 children) → returns true
4. Check node(5): leaf (0 children) → returns true
5. Node(2) receives true AND true → returns true
6. Check right subtree at node(3): has 2 children (6 and 7) → check both
7. Check node(6): leaf → returns true
8. Check node(7): leaf → returns true
9. Node(3) receives true AND true → returns true
10. Root(1) receives true AND true → returns true

examples.input: Tree: root(1) with left(2) and right(3); node 2 has left(4) only; node 3 has no children
examples.trace:
1. Start at root(1): has 2 children (2 and 3) → check both
2. Check node(2): has only left child (4) → 1 child → returns false immediately
3. Root receives false AND (whatever from right subtree) → false
4. Algorithm may still check node(3) but result is already determined as false

testCases.input: Root(1) with left(2) and right(3); both leaves
Root(1) with left(2) and right(3); node 2 has left(4) only; node 3 leaf
Perfect binary tree of height 2: root(1) with left(2) and right(3); node 2 has left(4) and right(5); node 3 has left(6) and right(7)
Root(1) with left(2) and right(3); node 2 has left(4) and right(5); node 3 has left(6) only
Root(1) with left(2) and right(3); node 2 has left(4) and right(5); node 4 has left(8) only
Root(1) with left(2) and right(3); node 2 has left(4) and right(5); node 3 has left(6) and right(7); all leaves have no children
Root(1) with left(2) and right(3); node 2 has left(4) and right(5); node 3 has no children

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Recursive Node Check Function
ladders.desc: The recursive function that checks a single node's children and recurses to subtrees.
ladders.explanation:
This sub-routine is the heart of the full solution—a recursive function that determines if the subtree rooted at a given node is full. The intuition is that we can define the full tree property recursively: a tree is full if its root has either 0 or 2 children, AND all subtrees are full. The function takes a node as input and returns a boolean. First, it handles the base case: if the node is null (empty subtree), we return true (an empty tree is trivially full). For non-null nodes, we check the number of children. If the node has 0 children (both left and right are null), it's a leaf and returns true. If it has exactly 1 child (one child is null but the other isn't), it violates the full tree property, so we return false immediately. If it has 2 children (both left and right are non-null), we must recursively check both subtrees and return the logical AND of their results. This ensures all nodes in the subtree satisfy the property. The function naturally propagates results upward through the recursion stack. Time complexity for this function on a subtree with m nodes is O(m), as it visits each node once. Space complexity is O(h) for the recursion stack. This connects to L0 by being the main function called from the root. Common mistakes include not handling the null case correctly, checking children in the wrong order, or using OR instead of AND for the two-child case. Real code pattern: a recursive function with if-else branches for 0, 1, and 2 children.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Function isFullSubtree(node):
2. If node is null: return true
3. If node.left is null AND node.right is null: return true
4. If node.left is null XOR node.right is null: return false
5. Return isFullSubtree(node.left) AND isFullSubtree(node.right)

examples.input: Node with value 1, left=null, right=null
examples.trace:
1. Node is not null
2. Check children: both left and right are null → 0 children
3. Return true (leaf node)

examples.input: Node with value 1, left=Node(2), right=null
examples.trace:
1. Node is not null
2. Check children: left is not null, right is null → exactly 1 child
3. Return false immediately (violates full tree property)

ladders.title: Core Logic — Checking Node Children Configuration
ladders.desc: The central technique of determining if a node has 0, 1, or 2 children and acting accordingly.
ladders.explanation:
This core logic involves examining a node's children to determine which case applies: leaf (0 children), invalid (1 child), or internal node (2 children). The intuition is that the full binary tree definition is based entirely on this per-node condition, so correctly classifying nodes is essential. For any given node, we look at its left and right child pointers. If both are null, the node has 0 children—this is a leaf node, which is always valid for a full tree. If exactly one is null (either left is null and right isn't, or right is null and left isn't), the node has 1 child—this violates the full tree property, so we should immediately flag it as invalid. If neither is null, the node has 2 children—this satisfies the immediate condition, but we must still check the subtrees. This logic is implemented with simple conditional checks, typically using if-else statements. The key insight is that we can make a local decision based solely on the node's immediate children, without needing to look deeper initially. This connects to L1 by being the decision-making part of the recursive function. Time complexity for this check is O(1) per node. Space complexity is O(1). Common mistakes include using AND instead of XOR for the 1-child check, or not handling null pointers safely. Real code pattern: check left and right pointers with null comparisons.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. If node is null: handle separately (base case)
2. Check if node.left is null AND node.right is null: → 0 children case
3. Check if (node.left is null AND node.right is not null) OR (node.left is not null AND node.right is null): → 1 child case
4. Otherwise: → 2 children case

examples.input: Node with left=null, right=null
examples.trace:
1. Check left: null
2. Check right: null
3. Both null → 0 children

examples.input: Node with left=Node(2), right=null
examples.trace:
1. Check left: not null
2. Check right: null
3. Exactly one is null → 1 child

ladders.title: Building Block — Tree Traversal with Recursion
ladders.desc: Using recursion to traverse all nodes in a binary tree.
ladders.explanation:
This building block involves traversing a binary tree recursively, which is essential for visiting every node to check the full tree property. The intuition is that recursion naturally mirrors the tree's hierarchical structure: each node can be processed by handling the current node and then recursively processing its subtrees. For a binary tree, we typically use a depth-first approach: start at the root, process the node, then recursively traverse the left subtree, then the right subtree (or in other orders like pre-order, in-order, post-order). In this context, we need to visit all nodes exactly once to check their children. The recursive function calls itself on the left and right children, with base cases to stop at null nodes. This ensures we cover the entire tree. The key insight is that recursion breaks down the problem into smaller subproblems (subtrees), making it easier to reason about. This connects to L2 by providing the mechanism to apply the children check to every node. Time complexity for traversing n nodes is O(n). Space complexity is O(h) for the recursion stack. Common mistakes include infinite recursion by missing base cases, or not handling both subtrees. Real code pattern: a recursive function that calls itself on left and right children.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Define function traverse(node):
2. If node is null: return (base case)
3. Process current node (e.g., check its children)
4. traverse(node.left)
5. traverse(node.right)

examples.input: Tree: root(1) with left(2) and right(3)
examples.trace:
1. Start at root(1): process node 1
2. Recursively traverse left subtree: go to node(2)
3. Process node 2
4. Node 2 has no children, so left and right traversals return immediately
5. Back to root, traverse right subtree: go to node(3)
6. Process node 3
7. Done

examples.input: Tree: root(1) with left(2) only
examples.trace:
1. Start at root(1): process node 1
2. Traverse left: go to node(2), process it
3. Node 2 has no children, return
4. Traverse right from root: null, return immediately
5. Done

testCases.input: Root with left and right children
Two-level tree: root with left and right; left has left child
testCases.expected: Visit root, left, left's left, right
Visit all nodes in DFS order

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Accessing Node Children in Binary Tree
ladders.desc: Fundamental operation of checking left and right child pointers of a tree node.
ladders.explanation:
This basic operation involves accessing the children of a binary tree node, which is essential for determining how many children it has. In a binary tree, each node typically has two references: left and right, which point to its left and right children, respectively. These references can be null if the child does not exist. The intuition is that to check the full tree property, we need to examine these pointers to count children. The operation is simple: for a given node, we look at node.left and node.right. If both are null, the node has 0 children. If one is null and the other isn't, it has 1 child. If neither is null, it has 2 children. This requires understanding how to dereference pointers or access object properties. In code, this is usually done with dot notation (e.g., node.left) or arrow notation. It's a constant-time operation. This connects to L3 by being the action performed during tree traversal at each node. Common mistakes include trying to access children on a null node (causing null pointer errors), or misunderstanding null values. Real code pattern: use if statements to check node.left and node.right against null.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Given a node object
2. Check if node is null: if so, handle appropriately
3. Access node.left to get left child reference
4. Access node.right to get right child reference
5. Compare these references to null to determine child count

examples.input: Node with left pointer to Node(2), right pointer null
examples.output: left child exists, right child null
examples.trace:
1. Node is not null
2. Check node.left: returns reference to Node(2) (not null)
3. Check node.right: returns null
4. Conclusion: has left child but not right

examples.input: Node with both pointers null
examples.trace:
1. Node is not null
2. Check node.left: null
3. Check node.right: null
4. Conclusion: 0 children

testCases.input: Node with left pointing to another node with children
Node with right pointing to leaf node
Node with left and right pointing to non-null nodes
Node with left=Node, right=Node, both leaves

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Binary Tree Node Structure
ladders.desc: Understanding the basic structure of a node in a binary tree.
ladders.explanation:
This foundation concept covers what a binary tree node is—the fundamental building block of binary trees. A binary tree node is a data structure that typically contains three parts: a value (or data) stored in the node, and two references (or pointers) to its left and right children. The value can be any data type (e.g., integer, string). The left and right references point to other binary tree nodes, forming the tree structure. If a node has no left child, the left reference is null (or None in Python). Similarly for the right child. This creates a hierarchical structure where each node can have at most two children. The intuition is that trees are recursive structures: each node can be seen as the root of its own subtree. Understanding this node structure is prerequisite to manipulating trees, such as checking children. This connects to L4 by defining what we access when we check node.left and node.right. Common mistakes include confusing nodes with values, or not understanding null references. Real code pattern: define a class with value, left, and right attributes.

Step-by-step explanation:
1. A binary tree node has:
   - value: stores data
   - left: reference to left child node (or null)
   - right: reference to right child node (or null)
2. Example: Node with value 5, left pointing to Node(3), right null
3. This represents a node with value 5, left child 3, no right child
4. Nodes are linked to form a tree: starting from a root node, follow references to traverse

examples.input: Node with value 10, left=null, right=null
examples.trace:
1. Create node with value 10
2. Set left reference to null (no left child)
3. Set right reference to null (no right child)
4. This is a leaf node in a tree

examples.input: Node with value 1, left points to Node(2), right points to Node(3)
examples.trace:
1. Create node with value 1
2. Set left reference to a node with value 2
3. Set right reference to a node with value 3
4. This node has two children: left child value 2, right child value 3

approaches.name: Approach 2: Iterative BFS Check
approaches.summary: Use a queue to perform a breadth-first traversal, checking each node's children as they are visited; if any node does not have 0 or 2 children, return false. This method processes nodes level by level, ensuring all nodes are inspected in a systematic order.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(w) where w is the maximum width of the tree (due to queue storage)

ladders.title: Full Problem — Iterative BFS Check for Full Binary Tree
ladders.desc: Complete solution using BFS queue traversal to check if every node has exactly 0 or 2 children
ladders.explanation: This solution implements a complete iterative BFS approach to determine if a binary tree is a full binary tree. The intuition is that a full binary tree requires every node to have either 0 children (leaf) or exactly 2 children (internal node). By performing a breadth-first traversal using a queue, we can systematically check each node's children as we encounter them. The algorithm starts by handling the edge case of an empty tree (which is considered a full binary tree). Then it initializes a queue with the root node. While the queue is not empty, we dequeue a node and examine its children. If a node has exactly 1 child (either left or right but not both), we immediately return false since this violates the full binary tree property. Otherwise, if the node has children (both left and right), we enqueue both children to continue the traversal. If the node has no children, we simply continue to the next node. The traversal continues until all nodes are processed or a violation is found. The time complexity is O(n) where n is the number of nodes, as we visit each node exactly once. The space complexity is O(w) where w is the maximum width of the tree, which corresponds to the maximum number of nodes at any level stored in the queue. This approach is efficient and systematic, ensuring we check every node in level order. Connection to L1: This full solution builds upon the BFS traversal pattern established in L1, adding the specific child-count checking logic. Common mistakes include forgetting to handle the empty tree case, incorrectly checking child conditions (e.g., checking for 0 OR 2 children instead of NOT 1 child), or not properly enqueuing children when both exist. Real code pattern: def isFullTree(root): if not root: return True; queue = [root]; while queue: node = queue.pop(0); left_exists = node.left is not None; right_exists = node.right is not None; if left_exists != right_exists: return False; if left_exists and right_exists: queue.append(node.left); queue.append(node.right); return True

examples.input: Tree: [1,2,3,4,5,null,7] (root=1, left=2, right=3; 2.left=4, 2.right=5; 3.left=null, 3.right=7)
examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize queue with root node 1. Queue: [1]. Step 2: Dequeue node 1. Check children: left=2 exists, right=3 exists → both exist, so enqueue both. Queue: [2,3]. Step 3: Dequeue node 2. Check children: left=4 exists, right=5 exists → both exist, enqueue both. Queue: [3,4,5]. Step 4: Dequeue node 3. Check children: left=null (doesn't exist), right=7 exists → only right exists (1 child) → violation! Return false.

examples.input: Tree: [1,2,3,4,5,6,7] (complete binary tree with 3 levels)
examples.trace: Step 1: Queue: [1]. Step 2: Dequeue 1: children 2 and 3 both exist → enqueue both. Queue: [2,3]. Step 3: Dequeue 2: children 4 and 5 both exist → enqueue both. Queue: [3,4,5]. Step 4: Dequeue 3: children 6 and 7 both exist → enqueue both. Queue: [4,5,6,7]. Step 5: Dequeue 4: no children → continue. Queue: [5,6,7]. Step 6: Dequeue 5: no children → continue. Queue: [6,7]. Step 7: Dequeue 6: no children → continue. Queue: [7]. Step 8: Dequeue 7: no children → continue. Queue empty → return true.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — BFS Traversal with Queue
ladders.desc: Implement breadth-first traversal of a binary tree using a queue data structure
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine focuses on the BFS traversal pattern using a queue, which is the fundamental structure for the full solution. The intuition behind BFS is to explore nodes level by level, starting from the root, then its children, then grandchildren, etc. This ensures we process nodes in the order they appear in the tree hierarchy. The algorithm begins by checking if the root is null (empty tree), in which case traversal is complete. Otherwise, we initialize a queue (typically using a list or deque) and enqueue the root node. Then we enter a loop that continues as long as the queue is not empty. In each iteration, we dequeue a node from the front of the queue, process it (in the full problem, this means checking its children), and then enqueue its non-null children to be processed later. For a binary tree, each node can have up to two children (left and right), so we check if each child exists and enqueue them if they do. The time complexity is O(n) since we visit each node exactly once. The space complexity is O(w) where w is the maximum width (number of nodes at the widest level), as the queue stores nodes level by level. Connection to L0: This BFS traversal provides the systematic node visitation framework that the full solution builds upon by adding the specific child-count checking logic. Connection to L2: This level implements the queue management that enables the level-order processing. Common mistakes include using a stack instead of a queue (which would give DFS), not handling the empty tree case, incorrectly managing queue operations (FIFO order), or forgetting to check if children exist before enqueuing them. Real code pattern: def bfs_traversal(root): if not root: return; queue = [root]; while queue: node = queue.pop(0); # process node here; if node.left: queue.append(node.left); if node.right: queue.append(node.right)

examples.trace: Step 1: Queue: [1]. Step 2: Dequeue 1 → process 1. Enqueue children 2 and 3. Queue: [2,3]. Step 3: Dequeue 2 → process 2. Enqueue children 4 and 5. Queue: [3,4,5]. Step 4: Dequeue 3 → process 3. Enqueue children 6 and 7. Queue: [4,5,6,7]. Step 5: Dequeue 4 → process 4. No children to enqueue. Queue: [5,6,7]. Step 6: Dequeue 5 → process 5. No children. Queue: [6,7]. Step 7: Dequeue 6 → process 6. No children. Queue: [7]. Step 8: Dequeue 7 → process 7. No children. Queue empty → traversal complete.

examples.input: Tree: [1,2,null,4,null] (root=1, left=2, right=null; 2.left=4, 2.right=null; 4.left=null, 4.right=null)
examples.trace: Step 1: Queue: [1]. Step 2: Dequeue 1 → process 1. Enqueue left child 2 (right is null). Queue: [2]. Step 3: Dequeue 2 → process 2. Enqueue left child 4 (right is null). Queue: [4]. Step 4: Dequeue 4 → process 4. No children to enqueue. Queue empty → traversal complete.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Checking Node Children Count
ladders.desc: Determine if a binary tree node has exactly 0 or 2 children (not 1)
ladders.explanation: This core logic focuses on the essential condition for a full binary tree: each node must have either 0 children (making it a leaf node) or exactly 2 children (making it an internal node). The intuition is that having exactly 1 child violates the full binary tree property. For any given node, we need to examine its left and right child pointers. The check can be implemented by evaluating the existence of these children. Let left_exists be true if node.left is not null, and right_exists be true if node.right is not null. The node satisfies the full binary tree condition if left_exists and right_exists are both true (2 children) or both false (0 children). In other words, they must be equal (left_exists == right_exists). If one is true and the other is false, the node has exactly 1 child, which is invalid. This boolean logic is efficient and avoids explicit counting. The time complexity for checking one node is O(1), and space complexity is O(1) as we only use a few boolean variables. Connection to L1: This checking logic is what gets applied to each node during the BFS traversal from L1. Connection to L3: This builds upon the ability to access a node's children from L3. Common mistakes include incorrectly implementing the check (e.g., checking if children count is 0 OR 2 instead of NOT 1), forgetting to handle null pointers when accessing children, or using complex conditional statements instead of simple boolean equality. Real code pattern: def check_node(node): left_exists = node.left is not None; right_exists = node.right is not None; return left_exists == right_exists

examples.input: Node with left=2, right=3 (both children exist)
examples.trace: Step 1: Check left child: exists (not null). Step 2: Check right child: exists (not null). Step 3: left_exists = true, right_exists = true. Step 4: Compare: true == true → true. Node has 2 children → valid.

examples.input: Node with left=null, right=null (no children)
examples.trace: Step 1: Check left child: null → doesn't exist. Step 2: Check right child: null → doesn't exist. Step 3: left_exists = false, right_exists = false. Step 4: Compare: false == false → true. Node has 0 children → valid.

examples.input: Node with left=4, right=null (only left child)
examples.trace: Step 1: Check left child: exists (not null). Step 2: Check right child: null → doesn't exist. Step 3: left_exists = true, right_exists = false. Step 4: Compare: true == false → false. Node has 1 child → invalid.

ladders.title: Building Block — Accessing Node Children in Binary Tree
ladders.desc: Retrieve left and right child pointers from a binary tree node
ladders.explanation: This building block focuses on how to access the children of a node in a binary tree data structure. In most implementations, a binary tree node contains at least three components: a value (data stored in the node), a left child pointer (reference to the left subtree), and a right child pointer (reference to the right subtree). The left and right pointers may be null (or None) if the child does not exist. Accessing children is fundamental because it allows us to traverse the tree and examine node relationships. The intuition is that each node serves as a gateway to its subtrees—following the left pointer leads to the left subtree, and following the right pointer leads to the right subtree. To access children, we simply use node.left and node.right (or equivalent syntax in your programming language). It's crucial to check for null values before dereferencing to avoid null pointer errors. For example, if node.left is null, it means the node has no left child. This operation has O(1) time complexity since it's just pointer access, and O(1) space complexity. Connection to L2: Accessing children is necessary to perform the child count check from L2. Connection to L4: This builds upon the basic node structure from L4. Common mistakes include assuming children exist without null checks (leading to runtime errors), confusing left and right children, or not understanding that child pointers can be null. Real code pattern: class TreeNode: def __init__(self, val): self.val = val; self.left = None; self.right = None. To access: left_child = node.left; right_child = node.right

examples.input: Node with value=10, left=Node(5), right=Node(15)
examples.output: Left child value=5, Right child value=15
examples.trace: Step 1: Given node with value 10. Step 2: Access node.left → returns reference to node with value 5. Step 3: Access node.right → returns reference to node with value 15. Step 4: Both children exist (not null).

examples.input: Node with value=20, left=null, right=Node(25)
examples.trace: Step 1: Given node with value 20. Step 2: Access node.left → returns null (no left child). Step 3: Access node.right → returns reference to node with value 25. Step 4: Left child doesn't exist, right child exists.

testCases.input: Node: val=12, left=Node(13), right=Node(14) (children have values)

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Queue Enqueue and Dequeue
ladders.desc: Add elements to the back of a queue and remove elements from the front
ladders.explanation: This basic operation covers the fundamental queue operations: enqueue (add to back) and dequeue (remove from front). A queue is a First-In-First-Out (FIFO) data structure, meaning the first element added is the first one removed. The intuition is similar to a real-world queue (line): people join at the back and leave from the front. Enqueue adds an element to the rear/tail of the queue. Dequeue removes and returns the element from the front/head of the queue. These operations are essential for BFS traversal because we need to process nodes in the order they are discovered: when we visit a node, we add its children to the back of the queue, ensuring they are processed after all nodes at the current level. In Python, a list can simulate a queue using append() for enqueue (add to back) and pop(0) for dequeue (remove from front), though for efficiency, collections.deque is preferred with append() and popleft(). The time complexity for enqueue is typically O(1) (amortized for list, O(1) for deque), and dequeue is O(1) for deque but O(n) for list.pop(0) due to shifting elements. Space complexity depends on the number of elements stored. Connection to L1: These queue operations enable the BFS traversal in L1. Connection to L5: This builds upon the abstract queue concept from L5. Common mistakes include using stack operations (push/pop) instead of queue operations, confusing front/back order, or not handling empty queue during dequeue. Real code pattern: Using list: queue = []; enqueue: queue.append(item); dequeue: item = queue.pop(0) (if queue not empty). Using deque: from collections import deque; queue = deque(); enqueue: queue.append(item); dequeue: item = queue.popleft()

examples.input: Start with empty queue, enqueue A, enqueue B, dequeue, enqueue C, dequeue, dequeue
examples.trace: Step 1: Queue: []. Step 2: Enqueue A → Queue: [A]. Step 3: Enqueue B → Queue: [A,B]. Step 4: Dequeue → remove A from front, return A. Queue: [B]. Step 5: Enqueue C → Queue: [B,C]. Step 6: Dequeue → remove B from front, return B. Queue: [C]. Step 7: Dequeue → remove C from front, return C. Queue: [].

examples.input: Start with queue [X,Y], dequeue, enqueue Z, dequeue, enqueue W
examples.output: Dequeued items: X, Y; Final queue: [Z,W]
examples.trace: Step 1: Initial queue: [X,Y]. Step 2: Dequeue → remove X from front, return X. Queue: [Y]. Step 3: Enqueue Z → Queue: [Y,Z]. Step 4: Dequeue → remove Y from front, return Y. Queue: [Z]. Step 5: Enqueue W → Queue: [Z,W]. Final queue: [Z,W].

testCases.input: Enqueue 5 to empty queue, then dequeue
Enqueue A, enqueue B, enqueue C, dequeue, dequeue
Enqueue X, dequeue, enqueue Y, dequeue
Start empty, enqueue 'cat', enqueue 'dog', dequeue, enqueue 'fish', dequeue
Enqueue true, enqueue false, dequeue, dequeue, enqueue true
Start [a,b,c], dequeue, dequeue, enqueue d, dequeue
testCases.expected: Dequeued: a then b then c, Queue: [d]

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — What is a Binary Tree?
ladders.desc: Understand the basic structure of a binary tree: nodes with up to two children
ladders.explanation: A binary tree is a fundamental data structure in computer science consisting of nodes connected in a hierarchical manner. Each node contains: 1) A value (or data) that stores information, 2) A left child pointer that references the left subtree (or null if no left child), and 3) A right child pointer that references the right subtree (or null if no right child). The topmost node is called the root. Nodes with no children are called leaves. The intuition is that a binary tree organizes data in a branching structure, similar to a family tree but with at most two children per parent. This structure allows efficient searching, insertion, and traversal in many algorithms. Key properties: It is acyclic (no cycles), and each node (except the root) has exactly one parent. The height is the number of edges from root to deepest leaf. Binary trees are used in many applications like expression parsing, Huffman coding, and binary search trees. Time complexity for basic operations often depends on height. Space complexity depends on number of nodes. Connection to L4: This foundation explains what nodes are, which are the elements we enqueue/dequeue in L4. Common mistakes include confusing binary trees with binary search trees (which have ordering constraints), not understanding null pointers represent missing children, or thinking nodes can have more than two children. Real code pattern: class TreeNode: def __init__(self, val): self.val = val; self.left = None; self.right = None. Example tree: root = TreeNode(1); root.left = TreeNode(2); root.right = TreeNode(3)

examples.input: Simple binary tree with root value 10, left child 5, right child 15
examples.trace: Step 1: Create root node with value 10. Step 2: Create left child node with value 5. Step 3: Set root.left to point to left child. Step 4: Create right child node with value 15. Step 5: Set root.right to point to right child. Step 6: Result: root (10) has two children: left (5) and right (15).

examples.input: Binary tree with root 1, left child 2 (which has left child 4), right child null
examples.output: Structure: Node(1) with left=Node(2), right=null; Node(2) with left=Node(4), right=null
examples.trace: Step 1: Create root node with value 1. Step 2: Create node with value 2. Step 3: Set root.left to node 2. Step 4: Set root.right to null (no right child). Step 5: Create node with value 4. Step 6: Set node 2.left to node 4. Step 7: Set node 2.right to null. Result: root->left->left chain.

testCases.expected: Root node with value 42, left=null, right=null

testCases.input: Root 1 with left child 2, right child 3
Root A with left child B only
Root X with right child Y only
Root true with left=false, right=true (boolean values)
Root 0 with left=-1, right=1 (negative and positive)

approaches.name: Approach 3: Iterative DFS with Stack
approaches.summary: Traverse the tree using an explicit stack to simulate depth-first search iteratively, checking each node's children; return false upon finding a node that is not full. This approach avoids recursion, using manual stack management for traversal.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(h) where h is the height of the tree (due to stack storage)

ladders.title: Full Problem — Iterative DFS to Check Full Binary Tree
ladders.desc: Complete iterative DFS solution using stack to determine if binary tree is full (every node has 0 or 2 children).

examples.input: Tree: root(1) with left=2, right=3; node 2 has left=4, right=5; node 3 has left=6, right=7; all leaves have no children
examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize stack = [1]. Step 2: Pop 1, left=2, right=3 (both not None, valid). Push 3, then 2 → stack = [3,2]. Step 3: Pop 2, left=4, right=5 (both not None, valid). Push 5, then 4 → stack = [3,5,4]. Step 4: Pop 4, left=None, right=None (both None, valid). No push → stack = [3,5]. Step 5: Pop 5, left=None, right=None (valid). No push → stack = [3]. Step 6: Pop 3, left=6, right=7 (both not None, valid). Push 7, then 6 → stack = [7,6]. Step 7: Pop 6, left=None, right=None (valid). No push → stack = [7]. Step 8: Pop 7, left=None, right=None (valid). Stack empty. Return True.

examples.input: Tree: root(1) with left=2, right=3; node 2 has left=4, right=None; node 4 has no children; node 3 has no children
examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize stack = [1]. Step 2: Pop 1, left=2, right=3 (both not None, valid). Push 3, then 2 → stack = [3,2]. Step 3: Pop 2, left=4, right=None (one None, one not None → invalid). Return False immediately.

testCases.input: root = Node(1) with left=Node(2), right=Node(3); node 2 has left=Node(4), right=Node(5); node 3 has left=Node(6), right=Node(7)
root = Node(1) with left=Node(2), right=Node(3); node 2 has left=Node(4), right=None; node 4 has no children; node 3 has no children
root = Node(1) with left=Node(2), right=Node(3); node 2 has left=Node(4), right=Node(5); node 3 has left=None, right=Node(6)
root = Node(1) with left=Node(2), right=Node(3); node 2 has left=Node(4), right=Node(5); node 3 has left=Node(6), right=Node(7); node 4 has left=Node(8), right=Node(9)
root = Node(1) with left=Node(2), right=None; node 2 has left=Node(3), right=Node(4)
root = Node(1) with left=Node(2), right=Node(3); node 2 has left=None, right=None; node 3 has left=None, right=None
root = Node(1) with left=Node(2), right=Node(3); node 2 has left=Node(4), right=Node(5); node 4 has left=None, right=Node(6)

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Stack-based DFS Traversal for Binary Tree
ladders.desc: Implement iterative DFS traversal using a stack to visit all nodes of a binary tree.

examples.input: Tree: root(1) with left=2, right=3; node 2 has left=4, right=None; node 4 has no children; node 3 has no children
examples.output: Traversal order: 1, 2, 4, 3 (if pushing right then left)
examples.trace: Step 1: stack = [1]. Step 2: pop 1, process 1. Push right=3, then left=2 → stack = [3,2]. Step 3: pop 2, process 2. Push right=None (skip), left=4 → stack = [3,4]. Step 4: pop 4, process 4. No children → stack = [3]. Step 5: pop 3, process 3. No children → stack empty. Traversal complete.

examples.input: Tree: root(5) with left=3, right=8; node 3 has left=1, right=4; node 8 has left=7, right=9
examples.trace: Step 1: stack = [5]. Step 2: pop 5, process 5. Push right=8, left=3 → stack = [8,3]. Step 3: pop 3, process 3. Push right=4, left=1 → stack = [8,4,1]. Step 4: pop 1, process 1. No children → stack = [8,4]. Step 5: pop 4, process 4. No children → stack = [8]. Step 6: pop 8, process 8. Push right=9, left=7 → stack = [9,7]. Step 7: pop 7, process 7. No children → stack = [9]. Step 8: pop 9, process 9. No children → stack empty.

testCases.input: root = Node(1) with left=Node(2), right=Node(3); node 2 has left=Node(4), right=None
root = Node(1) with left=Node(2), right=Node(3); node 2 has left=Node(4), right=Node(5); node 3 has left=Node(6), right=Node(7)
root = Node(1) with left=Node(2), right=Node(3); node 2 has left=None, right=Node(4); node 3 has left=Node(5), right=None
root = Node(1) with left=Node(2), right=Node(3); node 2 has left=Node(4), right=Node(5); node 4 has left=Node(6), right=None
root = Node(1) with left=Node(2), right=Node(3); node 2 has left=None, right=None; node 3 has left=None, right=None
root = Node(1) with left=Node(2), right=Node(3); node 2 has left=Node(4), right=Node(5); node 3 has left=Node(6), right=Node(7); node 4 has left=Node(8), right=Node(9)

ladders.title: Core Logic — Checking Node Fullness in Binary Tree
ladders.desc: Determine if a single binary tree node has 0 or 2 children (full node property).
ladders.explanation:
This core logic involves checking whether a given binary tree node satisfies the condition of being full: it must have either exactly 0 children (both left and right are None) or exactly 2 children (both left and right are not None). The intuition is that this is the fundamental property that defines a full binary tree; if every node in the tree passes this check, the entire tree is full. For a node, we examine its left and right child pointers. If both are None, it's a leaf node and valid. If both are not None, it's an internal node with two children and valid. If one is None and the other is not None, it's invalid because it has exactly one child. This check is performed during traversal in L1 and L0. The operation is O(1) time and O(1) space per node. This connects to L1 by being the processing step applied to each node during DFS traversal. Common mistakes include incorrect boolean logic (e.g., using OR instead of AND), misunderstanding 'not None' checks (must ensure both children exist, not just that they are truthy), or not handling the edge case where a node might have missing child attributes (but in standard binary trees, left/right are either Node or None). This logic is simple but critical for the overall algorithm.

Pseudocode:
function isFullNode(node):
    left = node.left
    right = node.right
    if (left is None and right is None) or (left is not None and right is not None):
        return True
    else:
        return False

Time: O(1)
Space: O(1)

examples.input: Node with left=None, right=None
examples.trace: Step 1: left is None, right is None. Step 2: Condition (None and None) is True, so return True.

examples.input: Node with left=Node(2), right=Node(3)
examples.trace: Step 1: left is not None, right is not None. Step 2: Condition (not None and not None) is True, so return True.

examples.input: Node with left=Node(2), right=None
examples.trace: Step 1: left is not None, right is None. Step 2: First condition (None and None) is False. Second condition (not None and not None) is False because right is None. Step 3: Else return False.

testCases.input: Node with left=Node(1), right=Node(2) where Node(1) has children, but only check this node
Node with left=Node(1), right=Node(2) and Node(1)=None, Node(2)=None (but they are objects, not None)
Node with left=Node(1), right=Node(2) where Node(1) is None incorrectly (but in practice, left would be None, not Node(None))
Node with left=Node(1), right=Node(2) and both are valid Node instances
Node with left=None, right=Node(1) where Node(1) has no children
Node with left=Node(1), right=Node(2) and Node(1) is None (impossible in standard structure, so treat as not None)

ladders.title: Building Block — Stack Operations for Tree Traversal
ladders.desc: Use stack push and pop operations to manage nodes during iterative tree traversal.
ladders.explanation:
This building block involves using a stack data structure to store and retrieve nodes in a last-in, first-out (LIFO) order, which is essential for simulating depth-first traversal iteratively. The intuition is that a stack allows us to mimic the call stack of recursive DFS: we push nodes to visit later and pop the most recently added node to process it, ensuring we go deep into the tree before backtracking. For binary trees, we start by pushing the root. When we pop a node, we process it and then push its children; by pushing the right child before the left (if using pre-order), we ensure left is processed first after popping due to LIFO. Key operations are push (add node to top of stack) and pop (remove and return top node). We also need to check if the stack is empty to terminate the loop. This mechanism provides O(1) time per push/pop and O(h) space for the stack. This connects to L2 by providing the means to apply the node check to each node in sequence. Common mistakes include using a queue instead of stack (which would give BFS), not handling empty stack correctly (leading to errors), or pushing nodes in wrong order affecting traversal order but not correctness for this problem. Understanding stack operations is fundamental for many iterative algorithms.

Pseudocode:
stack = []  # Initialize empty stack
stack.append(root)  # Push root
while len(stack) > 0:  # While stack not empty
    node = stack.pop()  # Pop top node
    # Process node
    if node.right: stack.append(node.right)  # Push right child
    if node.left: stack.append(node.left)  # Push left child

Time: O(n) for n operations
Space: O(h) for stack storage

examples.input: Start with root=Node(1), push root, then process as above
examples.output: Stack states: [1] → pop 1 → push right=3, left=2 → [3,2] → pop 2 → push left=4 → [3,4] → pop 4 → [3] → pop 3 → []
examples.trace: Step 1: stack = [1]. Step 2: pop 1, stack = []. Push right=3 → stack = [3]. Push left=2 → stack = [3,2]. Step 3: pop 2, stack = [3]. Push left=4 → stack = [3,4]. Step 4: pop 4, stack = [3]. Step 5: pop 3, stack = [].

examples.input: Root=Node(5) with left=3, right=8; push/pop as above
examples.output: Stack states: [5] → pop 5 → push 8,3 → [8,3] → pop 3 → push right=4, left=1 → [8,4,1] → pop 1 → [8,4] → pop 4 → [8] → pop 8 → push right=9, left=7 → [9,7] → pop 7 → [9] → pop 9 → []
examples.trace: Step 1: stack = [5]. Step 2: pop 5, push 8 then 3 → stack = [8,3]. Step 3: pop 3, push 4 then 1 → stack = [8,4,1]. Step 4: pop 1 → stack = [8,4]. Step 5: pop 4 → stack = [8]. Step 6: pop 8, push 9 then 7 → stack = [9,7]. Step 7: pop 7 → stack = [9]. Step 8: pop 9 → stack = [].

testCases.expected: First pop: 8, stack=[6,7]; second pop: 7, stack=[6]
After push: [16,17]; after pop: popped 17, stack=[16]

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Accessing Children of Binary Tree Node
ladders.desc: Retrieve left and right child pointers from a binary tree node.
ladders.explanation:
This basic operation involves accessing the left and right attributes of a binary tree node, which are typically stored as pointers or references to child nodes. In a standard binary tree implementation, each node has fields such as 'left' and 'right' that may contain either another node object or None (null) to indicate no child. The intuition is that to traverse or analyze a tree, we need to examine these connections. For example, given a node 'n', we get its left child with 'n.left' and right child with 'n.right'. This operation is fundamental because it allows us to navigate the tree structure. It runs in O(1) time and uses O(1) space. This connects to L3 by providing the children to push onto the stack during traversal. Common mistakes include assuming children exist without checking for None (leading to attribute errors), confusing left/right order, or not understanding that None represents absence of a child. In Python-style pseudocode, we use 'node.left' and 'node.right', ensuring they are defined in the node class. This operation is simple but crucial for any tree algorithm.

Pseudocode:
class Node:
    def __init__(self, val):
        self.val = val
        self.left = None
        self.right = None

# Accessing children:
left_child = node.left
right_child = node.right

Time: O(1)
Space: O(1)

examples.input: Node with value 10, left=Node(5), right=Node(15)
examples.trace: Step 1: node.val = 10. Step 2: node.left returns reference to Node(5). Step 3: node.right returns reference to Node(15).

examples.input: Node with value 20, left=None, right=Node(25)
examples.trace: Step 1: node.val = 20. Step 2: node.left returns None. Step 3: node.right returns reference to Node(25).

testCases.input: Node(9) with left=Node(10), right=Node(11) where Node(10) has children
testCases.expected: left=Node(10), right=Node(11) (children of Node(10) not accessed here)

testCases.input: Node(12) with left=Node(13), right=Node(14) and Node(13)=None (invalid, but treat as Node object)
Node(15) with left=Node(16), right=None where Node(16) has no children
Node(17) with left=None, right=Node(18) where Node(18) has left child
Node(19) with left=Node(20), right=Node(21) and both are leaf nodes
Node(22) with left=Node(23), right=Node(24) where Node(23) is None (impossible, so assume Node)

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Binary Tree Node Structure
ladders.desc: Understand the basic structure of a node in a binary tree.
ladders.explanation:
This foundation concept introduces the fundamental building block of a binary tree: the node. A binary tree node is a data structure that contains a value (or key) and two references: one to its left child and one to its right child. The intuition is that trees are composed of nodes linked together, where each node can have at most two children, forming a hierarchical structure. The node typically has attributes such as 'val' to store data, 'left' to point to the left child node (or None if no child), and 'right' to point to the right child node (or None if no child). This simple structure allows us to represent trees in code. For example, in Python, we might define a class Node with __init__ method setting these attributes. Understanding this is prerequisite because without nodes, there is no tree to traverse or check. It connects to L4 by providing the object whose children we access. Common mistakes include confusing nodes with values (the node is an object that contains a value), not initializing children to None, or misunderstanding that 'left' and 'right' are pointers (references) to other nodes, not the nodes themselves. This concept is essential for beginners to grasp before moving to more complex tree operations.

Pseudocode:
class Node:
    def __init__(self, value):
        self.val = value
        self.left = None
        self.right = None

# Example creation:
root = Node(1)
root.left = Node(2)
root.right = Node(3)

Time: O(1) to create a node
Space: O(1) per node

examples.input: Create a node with value 5
examples.output: Node object with val=5, left=None, right=None
examples.trace: Step 1: Call Node(5). Step 2: In __init__, set self.val = 5, self.left = None, self.right = None. Step 3: Return the node object.

examples.input: Create a small tree: root=10, left child=20, right child=30
examples.trace: Step 1: Create root = Node(10). Step 2: Create left_child = Node(20). Step 3: Set root.left = left_child. Step 4: Create right_child = Node(30). Step 5: Set root.right = right_child.

testCases.input: Node with value 2 and set left=Node(3)
Node with value 4 and set right=Node(5)
Node with value 6 and set left=Node(7), right=Node(8)
Node with value 10, left=Node(11) where Node(11) has val=11
Node with value 12, right=Node(13) where Node(13) has val=13
Node with value 14, left=Node(15), right=Node(16) with Node(15) val=15, Node(16) val=16
Node with value 17 and no children set
Node with value 18, left=Node(19), right=Node(20) and Node(19) has left child
testCases.expected: val=18, left=Node(19), right=Node(20) (Node(19) structure not detailed here)
